target.name: Mainspring Energy
target.founder: Shannon Miller, Adam Simpson, Matt Svrcek

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series E → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.4x) to $400M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 19 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
Mainspring Energy is a Series E climate and energy technology company that has raised $400M+ to date. The valuation universe for climate and energy comprises 1 public comparable (Lnza) and 8 private peers including TAE Technologies, Redwood Materials, Helion, Commonwealth Fusion Systems, and QuantumScape. However, the peer set lacks sufficient same-stage or adjacent-stage comparables with similar valuation characteristics to support a reliable peer-median approach—only 2 adjacent-stage peers were identified against a minimum threshold of 3—making cross-sectional benchmarking inapplicable.

The stage-progression method is the primary applicable framework, using the $400M total raised as a valuation proxy and applying a 1.4x step-up multiplier typical of Series E advancement. This yields an implied valuation of $560M. No additional triangulation methods produced independent datapoints, limiting the analysis to a single-method foundation.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $476M (low) to $644M (median $560M), derived from the stage-progression result and applied sensitivity bounds. The narrow methodological basis reflects structural constraints in the comparable set rather than analytical consensus. Key uncertainties include: the appropriateness of the 1.4x step multiplier for this specific company's capital efficiency and market conditions; capital structure and dilution effects not visible in total raised figures; and the absence of market-tested peer valuations in the immediate adjacent stages. Further analysis would benefit from recent funding data on the five top private peers or evidence of transaction multiples in frontier energy technology.
